item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It’s interesting - I deliberately wrote this without trying for consistency of effects, because I wanted to play with the idea, and because three logs of the same thing would be dull.

It wasn’t until I finished writing the D Class test (which was the last one I wrote) that I realised that there is consistency in how the room behaves, even if the precise effects are different. I would suggest there are two possible throughlines to be found, for those who (like me) appreciate internal logic.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is a well written story, and I loved the terrifying imagery. Buuuuut, I don't like how inconsistent the anomaly is. Every instance is so different from the other. I like SCPs to have a more internal logic, even if some of the variables are hidden or unexplained.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Absolutely outstanding horror, my dude. I fucking loved this.
+1
My only tiny, miniscule gripe is that I feel that maybe sending in a valuable MTF unit into a anomaly where no one was ever recovered seemed kind of, I don't know, wasteful? If that makes sense?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love this comment. Tactility is always the goal, especially in horror writing!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Pleased that you like it, Hippo - thanks. I went looking for ballet studio images early on, but couldn't find one that didn't contradict my description in some way. I think these might be the closest I could find:

https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Joffrey_ballet_school.jpg

https://www.pexels.com/photo/dance-studio-gym-indoors-sports-hall-270899/

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The current Log 2 was a late addition, so I can imagine it might not be working as well as the rest. Some of the early feedback was that the last log felt a little abrupt (and inconsistent) when it was the second log of two. Again, if there are ways to clarify things, I'd be very keen to do so.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I can understand this - that first log probably has the highest number of characters in simultaneous movement that I've ever tried to write. If you have any suggestions on how to make things clearer or more precise, let me know.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
When I get anxious at home and there's nothing else for me to do, I enjoy baking. One of my favourite things to bake is shortcrust pastry, because there's a very tactile sense of satisfaction I get from actually making it. Specifically, the part where you rub the butter into the dry ingredients; where you feel the cold grease of it mixing into the flour; where you can grab a handful of the mixture and just grab it in a fist and squeeze and feel butter melt and ooze out between the gaps in your fingers.

After having read the final addendum of this piece I don't think I'll ever think about that squeezing the same way again.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I quite like the changes you've made — I think my only complaint now is that this is one of those skips where an image would be absurdly easy to find (just the exterior of a studio apartment complex), and helps emphasize the immersion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The horror of the spatial anomaly is very well described and gets progressively worse with each addenda. +1 for sure

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is absolutely wild and I love it to pieces.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Great hopping hell, this is fantastic. The first log alone hits Instant Classic™ status for me. This is the sort of good ol'-fashioned nightmare logic we're in sore need of these days.

How is this only at +10? How can y'all be sleeping on this so hard???

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This has the logic of an all-too-familiar anxiety-fuelled dream: the kind of dream where you're trapped in place and find yourself unable to move quicker than a strolling pace, despite wanting to run or escape. This hits all the right (wrong) weirdness and imagery notes, but was hard to follow in places (specifically Log 2) despite its clear and consistent clinical tone. Log 3's slow-rising and increasingly claustrophobic "the walls are closing in" trope works effectively for sure — and there's extra points for a competent MTF too.

The build-up was only capitalized on at the end of Log 3, so you almost lost me for a bit, but this is a definite +1 for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm… Not sure how I feel about this. I'll probably upvote because it's well-written and I liked that disturbing ending. But at the same time, it was kinda hard for me to comprehend what was going on in that room, especially during the first Addendum. Because of all the names and weird action going on I nearly lost track of the events. But maybe I just wasn't reading carefully enough.

UPD: This actually grew on me after the first time I've read it. Great and very horrific thing. Solid updoot.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
In celebration of the end of the 2019 Cliche-Contest, here is what would have been my entry. I had actually started writing this before the contest came along, and although taylor_itkin was kind enough to allow it, I felt a bit bad about making it an entry. The relevant cliches should be obvious enough!

Many thanks to Zyn for feedback on the forum, and to Captain Kirby, Westrin and The Great Hippo for critique on Discord. Hippo, in particular, deserves gratitude for some of the best and most detailed feedback I've had in ages - thanks!
